subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Federal vs. State Responsibility for Environmental Policy
stimulus: Environmental regulation is a complex policy area that involves both the states and the federal government. Develop an argument that takes a position on whether the federal government should be primarily responsible for managing environmental policy or if it should be the responsibility of the states.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Brutus 1, The Federalist 10, The Preamble of the United States Constitution.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents — that requirement applies to the evidence row only.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: State governments are better at making environmental policy because they are closer to the people.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The federal government is better at making environmental policy because environment issues in one state can impact other states.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The federal government is better at making environmental policy because policy needs to be the same nationwide.

wrong_examples: There is a debate about whether the national or state governments should take the lead in environmental policy. (Describes a debate without taking a position — not a defensible claim)
Environmental policy is important. (No position, no line of reasoning — does not respond to the prompt)
common_traps: Do NOT require foundational documents in the thesis — that is a Row B requirement; a thesis that takes a clear position with a 'because' clause earns Row A regardless of whether it cites Brutus 1 or Federalist 10
A simple 'because X' clause establishes a line of reasoning — do not require a sophisticated multi-part argument for Row A
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for responding to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. Acceptable responses include: State governments are better at making environmental policy because they are closer to the people; the federal government is better at making environmental policy because environment issues in one state can impact other states; the federal government is better at making environmental policy because policy needs to be the same nationwide.

sample_responses.response: State governments are better at managing environmental policy because they are closer to the people and can tailor regulations to local conditions.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a defensible position (state governments) and establishes a line of reasoning (closer to the people, local tailoring). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The federal government is better at making environmental policy because environmental issues in one state can impact other states.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (federal government) with a logical line of reasoning (cross-state impact). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Environmental policy is an important issue that needs to be addressed by government.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a general truth without taking a defensible position or establishing a line of reasoning. Does not respond to the prompt. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A (thesis). Tier 3 requires at least one piece from a listed foundational document. Evidence points are cumulative — a response earning Tier 3 automatically earns Tiers 1 and 2.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt (does not need to support the thesis directly).

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic (does not need to support the thesis directly)
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions Brutus 1, Federalist 10, the Supremacy Clause, the Commerce Clause, the Tenth Amendment, the Preamble, or a relevant course concept.

wrong_examples: Evidence that is entirely off-topic (e.g., references to a foreign country's environmental policy with no connection to U.S. government concepts)
common_traps: At Tier 1, evidence only needs to be RELEVANT — it does not need to directly support the thesis; even evidence that is tangentially related to federalism or environmental policy earns Tier 1
Tier 1 does NOT require Row A — a student who earns no thesis points can still earn 1 evidence point if they identify a relevant piece of evidence
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for providing one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. Acceptable evidence includes: Brutus 1, The Federalist 10, the Preamble, the Necessary and Proper Clause, the Supremacy Clause, the Commerce Clause, the Tenth Amendment, the concept of federalism, or references to states as 'laboratories of democracy.'

sample_responses.response: The Tenth Amendment reserves powers not granted to the federal government to the states, which suggests states have constitutional authority over environmental policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides a relevant piece of evidence (Tenth Amendment) connected to the federalism question at the heart of the prompt. Earns Tier 1.

sample_responses.response: France has strong national environmental regulations that apply uniformly across the country.
sample_responses.commentary: Evidence from a foreign country's policy with no connection to U.S. constitutional or governmental concepts is not relevant to the prompt. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A (thesis) to be earned.

required_elements: One specific piece of evidence that directly supports the thesis, OR two pieces of relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Uses Brutus 1 to argue that a large national government cannot adequately address local environmental conditions, supporting a claim that states should control environmental policy.

wrong_examples: Lists evidence without connecting it to the thesis (e.g., 'Federalist 10 discusses factions' with no explanation of how this connects to environmental policy)
common_traps: Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A — if the thesis earns 0 points, evidence is capped at Tier 1 (1 point total)
Evidence must be specific — a vague reference to 'the Constitution' without naming a specific clause or concept does not meet the specificity requirement for Tier 2
scoring_points.official_rubric: The second evidence point is earned for using one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR for providing two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A (thesis). Acceptable response: Brutus 1 warns that a large national government cannot adequately address local conditions, supporting a claim that states should control environmental policy.

sample_responses.response: Brutus 1 argues that a large national republic cannot represent the diverse needs of all citizens. This supports the claim that states should control environmental policy because local governments are better positioned to address the specific environmental conditions of their regions.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides specific evidence from a foundational document (Brutus 1) and directly connects it to the thesis. Earns Tier 2 (assumes Row A is also earned).

sample_responses.response: The Constitution says the government should make policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not name a specific clause, amendment, or foundational document. A general reference to 'the Constitution' does not meet the specificity requirement for Tier 2. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Brutus 1, Federalist 10, or the Preamble). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from the listed foundational documents: Brutus 1, The Federalist 10, or the Preamble
alternatives.correct_example: Uses the Preamble's 'promote the general welfare' clause to argue the federal government must protect environmental health, AND uses the Commerce Clause to show federal authority over cross-state pollution — both directly supporting the thesis.

wrong_examples: Two pieces of evidence but neither from a listed foundational document (e.g., cites the Clean Air Act and EPA formation — relevant but not from the required list)
Evidence from a listed document that does not support the thesis (e.g., quotes Brutus 1 to argue against federal power but thesis argues FOR federal control)
common_traps: The foundational document must be from the LISTED documents (Brutus 1, Federalist 10, or the Preamble) — citing the Declaration of Independence or Federalist 51 does not satisfy this requirement for this FRQ
Both pieces must specifically support the thesis — evidence that is merely relevant to the topic but does not connect to the stated argument does not earn Tier 3
scoring_points.official_rubric: The third evidence point is earned for using TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from the listed foundational documents (Brutus 1, The Federalist 10, or the Preamble). Requires Row A. Acceptable response: Uses the Preamble's 'promote the general welfare' clause AND Federalist 10's argument about faction-control to support federal control of environmental policy.

sample_responses.response: The Preamble charges the federal government to 'promote the general welfare,' which includes protecting the environment for all citizens. Additionally, the Commerce Clause gives Congress authority to regulate interstate commerce — and since pollution crosses state lines, federal regulation is constitutionally justified.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis (Preamble + Commerce Clause), with one from the listed foundational documents (Preamble). Earns Tier 3 (assumes Row A is earned).

sample_responses.response: The Clean Air Act and the EPA were both created by the federal government to manage environmental policy, showing federal leadership in this area.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides relevant evidence but neither piece is from the required foundational document list (Brutus 1, Federalist 10, or the Preamble). Capped at Tier 2 at most — does not earn Tier 3. 0 pts for this tier.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis) and at least 2 points in Row B (evidence). Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the claim — not just restate it.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim with causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Brutus 1 would support the idea that states should be in charge of environmental policy because too much federal power will curtail liberty and fail to address local concerns about specific environmental conditions.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim with causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: Because the environment is central to 'promoting general welfare' through the health and well-being of citizens, the federal government should address environmental issues — state-by-state approaches would produce inconsistent protections.

wrong_examples: Brutus 1 supports the claim that states should control environmental policy. (Simply restates the connection without explaining HOW or WHY — must show causal or analytical logic)
As I said above, the evidence shows the federal government should lead. (Cross-references earlier content without providing reasoning)
common_traps: Reasoning must go BEYOND restating the evidence — it must explain the logical or causal chain from evidence → thesis; use words like 'because,' 'therefore,' 'this means that,' or 'as a result'
Requires Row A AND at least 2 evidence points — a student cannot earn Row C without both a thesis and substantial evidence
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for using reasoning to explain why the evidence supports the claim or thesis. Acceptable responses include: Brutus 1 would support the idea that states should be in charge of environmental policy because too much federal power will curtail liberty and fail to address local concerns; because the environment is central to 'promoting general welfare' through the health and well-being of citizens, the federal government should address environmental issues — state-by-state approaches would produce inconsistent protections.

sample_responses.response: Brutus 1 would support the idea that states should be in charge of environmental policy because too much federal power will curtail liberty and fail to address local concerns about specific environmental conditions.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the causal chain from Brutus 1's anti-federalist reasoning to the thesis that states should control environmental policy. Goes beyond restating evidence to explain why it supports the claim. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Brutus 1 supports the claim that states should control environmental policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Simply restates the connection between Brutus 1 and the thesis without explaining HOW or WHY Brutus 1 supports state control. Does not provide the required logical chain. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A (thesis). Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut, refute, or concede it. Simply identifying an opposing view without responding to it does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some people say that the federal government is better, but federal policies are 'one size fits all' and do not always meet local needs — states are better positioned to tailor environmental regulations to their unique geography and economy.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While some argue that states should control environmental policy because they know local conditions best, environmental problems like air and water pollution cross state lines, requiring federal coordination to be effective.

wrong_examples: Some argue that states should control environmental policy. (Only identifies the alternate perspective — does not rebut, refute, or concede it)
The other side is wrong. (Does not describe the alternate perspective or engage with it substantively)
common_traps: Must do MORE than just name the opposing view — must actively engage with it (explain why it is flawed, concede part of it while defending your thesis, or explain why your view is stronger)
Requires Row A (thesis) — a student without a thesis cannot earn Row D
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for responding to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Requires Row A. Acceptable responses include: Some people say that the federal government is better, but federal policies are 'one size fits all' and do not always meet local needs; while some argue that states should control environmental policy, the federal government has better resources and can coordinate cross-state environmental issues.

sample_responses.response: Some people say that the federal government is better at managing environmental policy, but federal policies are 'one size fits all' and do not always meet the unique local needs of individual states — states are better positioned to tailor regulations to their geography and local industries.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the opposing view (federal government better) and rebuts it with a specific reason (one-size-fits-all problem). Engages substantively with the alternate perspective. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: While some argue that states should control environmental policy, the federal government has better resources and can coordinate cross-state environmental issues that no single state could address alone.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the alternate view (state control) and rebuts it by pointing to federal coordination capacity for interstate problems. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Some argue that states should be in charge of environmental policy.
sample_responses.commentary: Only identifies the alternate perspective — does not rebut, refute, or concede it. Simply naming the other side earns 0 pts.
